items.title: F-1 Employment Authorization — Official
items.summary: All employment for F-1 students is prohibited unless explicitly authorized in advance by the DSO and/or USCIS. Unauthorized work is a severe status violation. Different work types have distinct rules, application windows, and limitations.

scope.failure_impact: SEVIS termination, deportation, future visa ineligibility, scholarship revocation, permanent bar from U.S. re-entry

details: Maximum 20 hours/week during academic sessions; full-time during official breaks.
Work must be physically on-campus or at an off-campus location educationally affiliated with the school.
Employment begins only after classes start and SEVIS registration is complete.
Must cease on-campus work if student withdraws or transfers.
failure_modes: Exceeding 20-hour limit during session
Working before program start date
Continuing work after SEVIS termination
mitigation: DSO consultation before accepting any offer
Time-tracking and self-auditing
Immediate reporting of employment to DSO for SEVIS update

details: CPT must be an integral part of the established curriculum (internship, practicum, co-op).
Requires a specific course for credit or a mandatory degree requirement.
Must have DSO authorization and a new I-20 with CPT notation before work begins.
Full-time CPT for 12 months or more eliminates eligibility for post-completion OPT.
common_violations: Beginning work before I-20 with CPT is issued
Working beyond dates authorized on I-20
CPT for a job unrelated to major field of study
controls: Apply for CPT at least 10 business days before intended start date
Ensure job offer letter meets DSO requirements
Verify CPT authorization dates match employment dates exactly

details: Pre-completion OPT: Limited to 20 hours/week while school is in session.
Post-completion OPT: Full-time after degree completion.
Requires USCIS Form I-765 filing fee and adjudication (90+ day processing).
Maximum 12 months of standard OPT per educational level.
Unemployment limit: 90 days total during standard OPT.
failure_modes: Starting work before receiving EAD card
Exceeding 90-day unemployment limit
Failure to report employment or address changes to DSO within 10 days
mitigation: Apply for OPT up to 90 days before program end date, no later than 60 days after
Maintain detailed records of all employment (I-983 for STEM OPT)
Set calendar alerts for reporting deadlines and unemployment day counter

details: Eligibility: Degree in DHS STEM-designated program, employer enrolled in E-Verify.
Requires formal training plan (Form I-983) signed by employer and student.
Must apply before initial 12-month OPT EAD expires.
Unemployment limit: 150 days total (including initial OPT period).
Mandatory self-evaluations and employer reporting to DSO every 6 months.
common_violations: Working for a non-E-Verify employer
Failing to submit timely I-983 evaluations
Material changes to training plan without DSO approval
controls: Confirm employer's E-Verify number before accepting offer
Submit STEM OPT application 90-100 days before current EAD expiry
Schedule bi-annual reporting reminders in calendar

details: Available only due to severe, unforeseen economic circumstances beyond student's control.
Requires demonstration of efforts to find on-campus work and severe need.
Must file Form I-765 with USCIS based on DSO recommendation and evidence.
Authorization is for off-campus work, up to 20 hours/week during sessions.
failure_modes: Insufficient documentary evidence of hardship
Working before USCIS approval
mitigation: Immediate consultation with DSO upon financial crisis
Document all circumstances (currency devaluation, sponsor loss, medical emergency)
Explore all other financial aid options first

details: Available if employment is with a recognized international organization (e.g., UN, IMF, World Bank).
Requires Form I-765 filing with USCIS and DSO recommendation.

links: Authority: U.S. DHS SEVP Policy Guidance: Practical Training
Authority: University DSO/International Office
Authority: USCIS Form I-765 Instructions
Authority: DHS STEM Designated Degree Program List
Authority: Ministry of Higher Education employment approval policy

items.title: I-20 Maintenance & Updates — Official
items.summary: The Form I-20 is the legal document certifying F-1 status and must be accurate, valid, and updated for all status-impacting changes. Students are responsible for requesting updates through DSO; failure to maintain current I-20 constitutes status violation.

scope.failure_impact: Loss of legal status, inability to travel/re-enter, employment authorization invalidation, scholarship suspension

details: Legal name changes must be reported with supporting documents (passport, marriage certificate).
U.S. residential address changes must be reported within 10 days of moving.
Emergency contact information must remain current in SEVIS and university systems.
failure_modes: I-20 name/passport mismatch causing travel denial
SEVIS termination for address non-compliance
University holds on registration
mitigation: Update address through university portal immediately upon move
Request updated I-20 after any legal name change
Quarterly review of all personal information in SEVIS

details: Required if unable to complete program by I-20 end date due to compelling academic or medical reasons.
Must apply before current I-20 expires with DSO recommendation.
Requires financial documentation showing continued support for extended period.
Academic advisor letter explaining delay necessity is mandatory.
common_violations: Applying after I-20 expiration date
Insufficient financial documentation for extension period
Continuing studies with expired I-20
controls: Monitor program progress against I-20 end date quarterly
Initiate extension request 60 days before expiration
Maintain communication with academic advisor about completion timeline

details: Must be accepted to new school before initiating transfer.
Must complete current term or obtain authorized early withdrawal.
Transfer release date must be coordinated between old and new DSOs.
Cannot begin classes at new school until transfer is complete in SEVIS.
failure_modes: Starting classes at new school before SEVIS transfer
Failure to obtain MoHE approval for transfer
Missing transfer release deadline (typically 60 days after program end)
mitigation: Obtain written MoHE approval before initiating transfer
Submit transfer request to current DSO 30+ days before intended start
Confirm SEVIS transfer completion with new DSO before registration

details: Travel signature on page 2 of I-20 must be less than 12 months old at re-entry (6 months for active OPT).
Signatures are valid for multiple entries within their validity period.
Emergency travel signatures may be available but require justification.
failure_modes: Denied boarding by airline due to expired signature
CBP denial of entry at port of entry
Stranded outside U.S. requiring emergency DSO contact
mitigation: Check travel signature validity before booking any international travel
Request new signature if current one expires within 30 days of planned return
Carry all previous I-20s as backup documentation

details: Significant changes in funding source (new sponsor, loss of funding) must be reported.
Updated financial documents showing ability to cover remaining program costs required.
MoHE-sponsored students must report any changes to both DSO and MoHE.
Changing educational level (Bachelor's to Master's) requires new I-20.
Substantial change in major may require new I-20 and financial documentation.
Change in thesis/dissertation topic that substantially alters program may require update.

links: Authority: U.S. DHS SEVP Policy Guidance: Maintaining Status
Authority: University DSO/International Office
Authority: Ministry of Higher Education transfer/extension policies
Authority: SEVIS Help Hub: Transferring Schools

items.title: Grace Periods & Status Termination — Official
items.summary: Grace periods provide limited lawful presence after program completion or status loss. Understanding triggers for status termination and restoration procedures is critical to avoid unlawful presence accrual.

scope.failure_impact: Unlawful presence accrual, 3/10-year re-entry bars, scholarship forfeiture, deportation proceedings

timeline.T0: Program completion date or status violation
timeline.T+15 days: DSO must report termination in SEVIS after violation
timeline.T+0 days (Violation): Unlawful presence begins immediately upon termination

details: 60-day period to depart U.S., transfer schools, or change status after program completion.
Program completion date is earlier of: final exam date, thesis defense date, or I-20 end date.
Cannot work during grace period except if on authorized OPT.
Travel outside U.S. during grace period ends the period; cannot re-enter.
failure_modes: Overstaying beyond 60 days
Attempting to re-enter during grace period
Beginning new program after grace period expires
mitigation: Mark calendar with exact grace period end date
Initiate transfer/change of status applications well before grace period ends
Confirm departure flight booked before grace period expiration

details: 30-day period for travel and departure after program end date on DS-2019.
Cannot work or study during this period.
Time cannot be extended for any reason.
Failure to maintain full course load without authorization
Unauthorized employment (including unpaid internships)
Failure to report address change within 10 days
Failure to extend I-20/DS-2019 before expiration
Failure to enroll in classes by program start date
Substantial violation of school conduct policies leading to dismissal
common_violations: Dropping below full-time without DSO approval
Working on-campus more than 20 hours/week
Forgetting to request program extension

details: Available if: violation was beyond student's control, student intends to complete studies, has not worked illegally, and is not deportable.
Must file Form I-539 with USCIS with supporting evidence and fee.
Processing time 6-12 months; cannot travel or work while application pending.
Cannot apply if out of status more than 5 months unless exceptional circumstances.
failure_modes: USCIS denial leading to accrual of unlawful presence
Applying after 5-month deadline without exceptional circumstances
Traveling while reinstatement pending (abandons application)
mitigation: Consult DSO immediately upon discovering status violation
Document all evidence showing violation was beyond control
Consider departing U.S. and re-entering with new I-20 if eligible

details: More restrictive than F-1; requires sponsoring agency recommendation.
Must apply within 30 days of status violation discovery.
Requires showing violation was minor or technical and beyond scholar's control.
Departing U.S. after status termination stops unlawful presence accrual.
May apply for new visa after departure but must disclose previous violation.
Re-entry bar periods: 180 days unlawful presence = 3-year bar; 365+ days = 10-year bar.

links: Authority: 8 CFR 214.2(f)(5) - F-1 grace periods
Authority: 22 CFR 62.22 - J-1 grace periods
Authority: USCIS Policy Manual: F-1 Reinstatement
Authority: University DSO/International Office
Authority: Ministry of Higher Education grace period policies

items.title: Travel & Re-entry Rules — Official
items.summary: International travel while maintaining F-1/J-1 status requires specific documentation validation and timing considerations. Re-entry is never guaranteed and is at CBP discretion. Advance preparation is mandatory.

scope.failure_impact: Denied entry to U.S., SEVIS termination, stranded outside U.S., scholarship interruption

timeline.Within 10 days: Report return to DSO if required

details: Valid passport (6+ months validity for most countries, including Oman)
Valid U.S. visa stamp (except for automatic revalidation cases)
Current I-20 with valid travel signature (F-1) or DS-2019 with travel validation (J-1)
Proof of financial support (current bank statements, sponsor letter)
Proof of enrollment/employment (transcript, enrollment verification, employment letter for OPT)
I-94 record from previous entry
failure_modes: Expired visa stamp (except automatic revalidation)
I-20 travel signature >12 months old (>6 months for OPT)
Insufficient financial documentation
Passport expiry within 6 months
mitigation: Create travel document checklist and verify each item 72 hours before departure
Request new travel signatures if current will expire during travel
Carry both physical and digital copies of all documents

details: Must apply at U.S. Embassy/Consulate outside U.S., typically in home country (Oman).
Requires visa interview appointment and all supporting documents.
Administrative processing (security checks) can add weeks/months to processing.
Visa denial does not affect current status if still inside U.S.
common_violations: Applying for visa in third country without strong ties
Insufficient documentation of continued ties to home country
Misrepresenting employment or study plans
controls: Schedule visa appointment well in advance of planned travel
Prepare updated evidence of MoHE sponsorship and intent to return to Oman
Consult with DSO about timing and documentation before visa appointment

details: Allows re-entry with expired visa when traveling to contiguous territory (Canada, Mexico, adjacent islands) for <30 days.
Requirements: Valid I-20/DS-2019 with travel signature, valid passport, valid I-94, not applying for new visa during trip.
Not available if: from designated state sponsor of terrorism country (does not include Oman), previously denied visa, or out of status.
Cannot visit other countries beyond contiguous territory during trip.
failure_modes: Applying for new visa while in contiguous territory
Staying beyond 30 days
Visiting non-contiguous countries during trip
mitigation: Print and carry automatic revalidation regulations
Confirm all eligibility criteria before travel
Have DSO contact information available at port of entry

details: Must have: valid passport, valid visa (unless automatic revalidation), EAD card, I-20 with valid travel signature (<6 months old), job offer letter or employment verification.
Unemployed OPT students may be denied re-entry if exceeding 90-day unemployment limit.
STEM OPT requires I-20 with current employer information and valid travel signature.
failure_modes: Traveling while unemployed on OPT beyond 90-day limit
Missing employment verification letter
EAD card lost/stolen while abroad
controls: Obtain employment verification letter before travel
Calculate unemployment days before departure
Carry multiple copies of EAD card separately

details: Permitted during summer/winter breaks if returning for next term.
Must be enrolled full-time before departure and intending to enroll full-time upon return.
Final semester travel is high-risk; consider completing all requirements before travel.
Death/illness in family requires: proof of emergency, DSO consultation, potential expedited travel signature.
Document all communications with DSO about emergency.
Consider impact on exams, assignments, and enrollment status.

links: Authority: U.S. CBP Travel Requirements for F/J Students
Authority: U.S. Department of State Visa Information
Authority: Automatic Revalidation - CBP Website
Authority: University DSO Travel Advisory
Authority: Ministry of Higher Education travel approval procedures

items.title: J-1 Two-Year Home Residency Requirement — Official
items.summary: Section 212(e) of the Immigration and Nationality Act mandates that certain J-1 exchange visitors return to their home country for two years after program completion before being eligible for certain U.S. immigration benefits. Determination is made at visa issuance.

scope.failure_impact: Ineligible for H/L/K visas, immigrant visas, or change of status to most nonimmigrant categories; must fulfill requirement or obtain waiver

details: Government-funded: Receiving funding from U.S. or home country government (including MoHE).
Skills list: Field appears on Exchange Visitor Skills List for home country (Oman maintains list).
Graduate medical education: Receiving medical training in U.S.
Check DS-2019 box 2 to see if subject to requirement.
failure_modes: Misinterpreting DS-2019 notation
Assuming no requirement without verification
Accepting barred immigration benefit and being denied
mitigation: Review DS-2019 box 2 upon receipt
Consult with J-1 program sponsor for clarification
Verify with MoHE if funding triggers requirement

details: Cannot change status inside U.S. to H, L, or K nonimmigrant categories.
Cannot obtain immigrant visa or adjustment of status to permanent resident.
Can return on B, F, or new J visa during 2-year period.
Time outside home country does not count toward 2-year requirement.
common_violations: Applying for H-1B while subject to requirement
Marrying U.S. citizen and applying for adjustment without waiver
Attempting to change to F-1 without waiver if subject

details: No objection statement: Omani government (through MoFA) submits no objection to U.S. Department of State.
Exceptional hardship: To U.S. citizen or permanent resident spouse/child.
Persecution: Reasonable fear of persecution in home country.
Interested government agency: U.S. federal agency requests waiver.
Conrad State 30: For physicians working in underserved areas.
authorization_sources: U.S. Department of State Waiver Review Division
failure_modes: Incomplete waiver application leading to denial
Applying for waiver without consulting MoHE
Beginning new immigration process before waiver approval
mitigation: Consult with immigration attorney experienced in J-1 waivers
Obtain MoHE approval before pursuing waiver
Submit complete documentation with waiver application

details: Physical presence in Oman for aggregate of 2 years after program completion.
Short trips abroad (<30 days) generally permitted but consult with attorney.
Document proof of physical presence (passport stamps, employment records, residence proofs).
MoHE employment in Oman counts toward requirement.
controls: Maintain detailed records of all time spent in Oman
Obtain employment verification letters from Omani employers
Consult with immigration attorney before applying for barred benefits

details: J-2 dependents are subject to same 2-year requirement if J-1 principal is subject.
J-2 waiver must be applied for separately but typically granted with J-1 waiver.
J-2 children who turn 21 may need separate waiver evaluation.

links: Authority: Immigration and Nationality Act Section 212(e)
Authority: U.S. Department of State J-1 Waiver Information
Authority: Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Oman
Authority: Ministry of Higher Education J-1 policies
Authority: Exchange Visitor Skills List - Federal Register

items.title: Dependent Status (F-2/J-2) — Official
items.summary: Spouses and unmarried children under 21 may accompany F-1/J-1 holders as dependents. Their status is derivative and contingent on principal's status. Specific rules govern their activities and benefits in U.S.

scope.failure_impact: Dependent status termination, unlawful presence accrual, separation from family, immigration violations

timeline.Within 30 days: School enrollment for children (varies by state)

details: Cannot work in any capacity, paid or unpaid.
Can study part-time at SEVP-certified institutions (full-time requires change to F-1).
May engage in volunteer activities that are truly voluntary (not displacing paid workers).
Must maintain health insurance as required by university/policy.
Status automatically terminates if F-1 principal loses status.
common_violations: F-2 spouse engaging in unpaid internship
F-2 child attending public school full-time without change of status
F-2 dependent remaining in U.S. after principal departs
mitigation: Clear understanding of employment prohibitions
Consult DSO before any study enrollment
Coordinate travel with principal's status maintenance

details: May study full-time or part-time without restriction.
May apply for work authorization from USCIS (Form I-765) for self-support, not principal.
Employment must not be necessary for principal's support.
Can engage in volunteer activities.
Status contingent on J-1 principal maintaining status.
failure_modes: Working without EAD approval
Employment income used to support J-1 principal
Failing to maintain health insurance
controls: Apply for work authorization 90+ days in advance of desired start date
Maintain separate finances showing self-support
Carry proof of health insurance at all times

details: F-2/J-2 children may attend K-12 public schools full-time.
F-2 adults/children may attend college/university part-time; full-time requires status change.
J-2 dependents may study full-time without restriction.
Some states require immunizations and residency proofs for K-12 enrollment.
mitigation: Research school district enrollment requirements before arrival
Obtain school records and immunization records from Oman
Consult with DSO/RO before college enrollment for F-2s

details: Must have own visa stamp (unless Canadian citizen).
F-2: Need principal's valid I-20 with dependent listing and travel signature.
J-2: Need principal's DS-2019 with dependent listing and travel validation.
Cannot travel without principal unless for very short periods with documentation.
failure_modes: Traveling with expired dependent visa
Traveling without principal's signed document
Re-entering after principal has completed program

details: F-2 to F-1: Must apply for change of status to study full-time.
J-2 to F-1: Possible but complex due to potential 212(e) applicability.
Upon principal's change: Dependents must change status concurrently or depart.
Divorce/separation: Dependent status terminates; must change status or depart.

links: Authority: 8 CFR 214.2(f) - F-2 regulations
Authority: 22 CFR Part 62 - J-2 regulations
Authority: USCIS Form I-765 Instructions for J-2
Authority: University DSO/International Office
Authority: Ministry of Higher Education dependent policies

items.title: Change of Status Procedures — Official
items.summary: Changing from one nonimmigrant category to another (e.g., F-1 to H-1B) requires careful planning, timing, and adherence to specific procedures. Some changes can be done within U.S., others require consular processing. Unauthorized change attempts constitute status violation.

scope.failure_impact: Status gap leading to unlawful presence, employment authorization loss, deportation risk, future immigration benefit denial

timeline.T-90 days: File change of status application (varies by category)

details: H-1B petition must be filed while F-1 status is valid.
Cap-gap provision automatically extends F-1 status and OPT work authorization until October 1 if selected.
If not selected: Must depart by end of 60-day grace period or maintain other status.
Change occurs on October 1 when H-1B status begins.
failure_modes: H-1B petition filed after OPT expiration
Traveling during cap-gap period without proper documentation
Assuming H-1B approval before October 1
mitigation: Coordinate with employer's immigration attorney on timing
Obtain updated I-20 with cap-gap notation from DSO
Avoid international travel between April 1 and October 1 unless necessary

details: F-1 to J-1: Possible but may have 212(e) implications.
J-1 to F-1: Requires waiver if subject to 2-year home residency requirement.
To B-1/B-2: For temporary stay after studies; cannot study or work.
To O-1: For individuals with extraordinary ability; high evidence threshold.
common_violations: Beginning work in new status before approval
Continuing studies after applying for change to non-student status
Overstaying while change application pending

bullets.rule: Change of Status Within U.S. (Form I-539)
details: Must file before current status expires.
Can remain in U.S. while application pending but cannot work or study unless specifically authorized.
Filing fee required; premium processing available for some categories.
Denial results in accrual of unlawful presence from original status expiration.
failure_modes: Filing after status expiration (only allowed within 30 days with good cause)
Departing U.S. while application pending (abandons application)
Assuming continued authorization during pending period
mitigation: File application at least 45 days before status expiration
Maintain copies of filing receipt and all supporting documents
Do not travel internationally until status change is approved

details: Departing U.S. and applying for new visa category at consulate abroad.
Advantage: Faster processing in some cases, guaranteed status change date.
Risk: Visa denial leaves you outside U.S., re-entry not guaranteed.
Required for some categories that cannot change status within U.S.
controls: Confirm eligibility for consular processing before departing U.S.
Prepare complete visa application packet before leaving
Have backup plan if visa is denied or delayed

links: Authority: USCIS Form I-539 Instructions
Authority: USCIS H-1B Cap-Gap Guidance
Authority: U.S. Department of State Visa Processing
Authority: University DSO/International Office
Authority: Ministry of Higher Education status change approval
